reviews.review_msg: His lecture slides are alright; the biggest problem is the structure of the course. How do we have a quiz on a topic we just learned the same day? If you have a 2-hour lecture and covered 5 complicated diagrams, well, good luck because in 10 minutes you have a quiz on it. He also does not post the slides, so you can study before.

Taught the easiest content in the most inefficient and infuriating way possible. 

Lectures were monotonous and contained the exact same information as the weekly textbook reading. Exams were full of typos and mistakes. My group project had 10 people in it, and final grades relied heavily on peer feedback (how can you fairly review 10 people?).

This prof is okay. He seems pretty impatient, even though he makes processes like distributing exam booklets really inefficient and difficult for no reason. His lectures alright and he explains concepts well. The group assignments are a pain, no devices allowed for research, and the groups are too big to get anything done…

He showed the criteria for the semester final group project on the afternoon the day it was due. We had to refilm and re edit the video asap because of that. There were no clear outlines or dates for most of things too. I asked him a question only one time during the semester and he responded rudely

James is the nicest PERSON, however his exams are incredibly tough, especially the law exam. He's very set on rules when taking canvas quizzes and the exams are worse. You have to write essays… the class was fun, but the teaching wasn't great. He isn't ideal for lectures, but it is doable.

Group Project: Extremely disorganized 
Canvas: Very confusing
Final: Hardest final I have ever taken, questions are not testing you if you know definition, their testing you based on actual scenarios which are technically if you not a business student, horrible. 
Slides: You dont understand anything, slides make you even more confuse

Overcomplicated entry level course Weekly activities with banned electronics(for research task...) required handwritten submissions that we had to email. It felt like he tried to make processes as uncomfortable as possible.
exams were disorganized, 30min wait to get papers
stupid rules and incorrect course schedule.

He mentioned that it was his first time teaching BUS 200. It was a 3 hour class and we alomost always end early, the structure of his classes were one week there would be a lecture ex. ch1 and the next week would be a lecture about ch2 5 mins after that lecture we would have a quiz about what we learned in ch1 and what we just learned in ch2

Dr. Pflanz was my first BUS prof, and I genuinely enjoyed his class over the summer so much that I took another course with him this fall! His tests were quite intense, but he's super sweet if you get to know him, lowk funny and his experience as a lawyer makes him a great resource for those aspiring. Very sociable and gives really good feedback

One of my favourite instructors I've had so far, James makes sure to keep the class as engaged as possible by providing examples from his own life as a lawyer and even provide hints on how to do well in his exams. To succeed in his class, get ready to do lots of practice case questions.

So James lectures are based out of the modules he presents, you will have to read through text material if you are looking for good grades. He's definitely able to create a real connection of the law with examples from corporate business structures. There's a lot to memorize, think like a corporate lawyer.

Easily my favourite professor throughout 4 years of Beedie. Hilarious, like laughing out loud in class hilarious. 

There is a lot to memorize, you need to read the textbook every week. But James' lectures & anecdotes will clear any confusion.

I really enjoyed BUS233 with Professor Pflanz. His business law content is detailed and informative, the exam is fair and covers strictly what he went over in class. It is definitely a course where you need to put the time in, and memorize material in order to succeed. His legal background makes it cool. Law half was better than ethics.

This was one of my favourite prof at SFU. For a first law class I really enjoyed his course structure. Slides are based on textbook and the course is very organized. Be prepared for a lot of memorization so I recommend to understand concept and also some active recall. His lectures are clear and concise, and we can see that his passionate!

For the students who say he is sometimes rude - it's not malicious - keep in mind that Pflanz is first and foremost a real practicing lawyer, and second, a lecturer at SFU. He deals mostly with real-world legal issues and actual clients, whereas SFU keeps their students in a bubble. It comes from a place of wisdom, experience, and some humor :)
